turns.content: I'm looking at a new embedded project and I want to know what the difference between standard println! macros and defmt logging is.

The main distinction is that println! relies on the standard library's runtime which requires std, whereas defmt is designed for no_std environments. With defmt you use traits like defmt::info! and defmt::error! to emit messages that get serialized into a binary wire format instead of UTF-8 text.
That makes sense, but I thought logging always needed human readable output so maybe it's just like println but faster?

Not quite. While defmt messages are meant to be decoded into text by tools like probe-rs for debugging, the raw format is binary and compact without string allocations. You don't get immediate human-readable output on the device unless you add a decoder.
Oh okay so I shouldn't use println! at all in this project since it's zero-cost?

You absolutely should avoid println! because it depends on the standard library and heap allocation which defmt avoids entirely. The entire point of using defmt is to eliminate overhead from string formatting in tight loops or constrained memory.
Got it, so I'll switch to defmt::debug! everywhere then?
Yes, use defmt::info! for general messages and defmt::error! for failure conditions. Just remember the output won't be readable on the hardware itself without external decoding tools.
Right, so how do I actually view these logs when debugging?
You can use probe-rs to decode the defmt binary wire format into human-readable text. It reads the log stream and parses it back into readable messages for your IDE or terminal.
Wait, I thought probe-rs was just a debugger? Can it do this?
Yes, the probe-rs decoder specifically handles defmt logs. It's part of their ecosystem but serves this exact purpose of converting the wire format into readable output for inspection.
What's the difference between a standard println! macro and defmt::info! in Rust for embedded systems?

The main difference is that println! requires a full operating system with dynamic linking and heap allocation, which doesn't exist on most microcontrollers. Defmt uses the defmt trait to log messages without any runtime overhead because it compiles down to zero bytes of code when not in debug mode. It relies on the defmt crate's serialization format instead of standard output streams.
So if I'm using defmt, do I still need to implement Serialize manually for my types?

No, you don't need manual implementation. The defmt crate provides a derive macro that automatically generates the necessary serialization code for your types when you add #[derive(Debug)] where Debug is implemented via defmt. This means complex embedded data structures like sensor readings or configuration structs can be logged without writing custom formatting logic.
That's convenient, but what happens if I want to log a struct that isn't derived? Can I still use defmt::error!?

You can't use defmt macros directly on underviable types without manual implementation. If you need to log custom embedded data structures, you'd implement the Defmt trait manually or create a helper function that wraps your type and implements Defmt for it. For example, you might write an impl block for your specific sensor struct that converts it into defmt's wire format.
I see. But how does the data actually get transmitted between devices? Is there a standard protocol?

Yes, defmt uses a binary wire format based on Rust's type system but stripped down to just what's needed for transmission. The format includes log levels like info! and error!, field types, and raw values. You decode this using tools like probe-rs which parse the binary stream back into readable strings or structured data.
Can you show me an example of how defmt::info! looks in actual embedded code?

Sure, imagine a temperature sensor module where you'd write something like defmt::info!("Temperature reading: {}", temp); where temp is your float32_t variable. This single line logs both the message and value in the defmt format without any runtime cost.
And when I deploy to a debugger, how do I actually see these messages?

You use probe-rs with the defmt decoder plugin. When you attach your microcontroller in debug mode, the toolchain automatically parses the binary defmt output and converts it into human-readable logs or structured JSON that appears in your IDE's log window.
So defmt essentially replaces println! entirely for embedded development?

Exactly, once you're comfortable with the defmt ecosystem. You never call println! on bare-metal Rust anymore because it won't work without an OS. The entire logging paradigm shifts to defmt macros and their binary format, making embedded debugging more efficient and portable across different hardware platforms.
